title: Charmed Life - Chapter 17 (Fragment 3)
text:
Cat said, “But I haven’t any talents,” and Janet said at the same time, “How rare are these people?”

“Extremely rare,” said Chrestomanci. “Apart from Cat, the only other person with nine lives that I know of on this world is myself.”

“Really?” Cat was pleased and interested. “Nine?”

“I did have nine. I’ve only got two now. I was even more careless than Cat,” Chrestomanci said. He sounded a little ashamed. “Now I have to take care to keep each life separately in the safest place I can think of. I advise Cat to do the same.”

Janet’s ready brain promptly got to work on this. “Is one life here and the other downstairs having supper at this moment?”

Chrestomanci laughed. “It doesn’t work like that. I—”

To Janet’s disappointment, Euphemia hurried in with a tray and prevented Chrestomanci explaining how it did work. Mr. Saunders came in on Euphemia’s heels, still unable to find evening clothes that covered his wrists and ankles.

“Is he all right?” Euphemia asked anxiously. “My Will was uttering threats, but if it was him I’ll never speak to him again. And whatever happened to this carpet?”

Mr. Saunders was looking at the wrinkled and heaped-up carpet too. “What did it?” he said. “There were surely enough charms in this carpet to stop any kind of accident.”

“I know,” said Chrestomanci. “But this was amazingly strong.” The two of them looked at one another significantly.

Then everyone fussed over Cat. He had a most enjoyable time. Mr. Saunders sat him up on pillows, and Euphemia put him in a nightshirt and then stroked Cat’s head, just as if he had never confessed to turning her into a frog. “It wasn’t Will,” Cat said to her. “It was me.” Chrestomanci gave him a fierce swig of brandy and then made him drink a cup of sweet tea. Janet had a cup of tea too, and felt much better for it. Mr. Saunders helped Euphemia straighten the carpet, and then asked if he should strengthen the charms in it.

“Dragons’ blood might do the trick,” he suggested.

“Frankly, I don’t think anything will,” said Chrestomanci. “Leave it.” He got up and turned the mirror straight. “Do you mind sleeping tonight in Cat’s room?” he asked Janet. “I want to be able to keep an eye on Cat.”

Janet looked from the mirror to Chrestomanci, and her face became very pink. “Er,” she said. “I’ve been making faces—”

Chrestomanci laughed. Mr. Saunders was so amused that he had to sit on the blue velvet stool. “I suppose it serves me right,” said Chrestomanci. “Some of the faces were highly original.”

Janet laughed too, a little foolishly.

Cat lay, feeling comfortable and almost cheerful. For a while, everyone was there, settling him in. Then there seemed only to be Janet, talking as usual.

“I’m so glad you’re all right,” she said. “Why did I open my big mouth about those matches? I had the dreaded umjams when you suddenly flared up, and when the carpet didn’t work, the only thing I could think of was to yell for Chrestomanci. I was right. He came before the words were out of my mouth, even though the door was locked. It was still locked when he opened it, but the lock isn’t broken, because I tried it. So he is an enchanter. And he ruined a suit over you, Cat, and didn’t seem to mind, so I think that when he isn’t being like freezing fog over the Grampians, he’s really very nice. This isn’t for the benefit of the mirror. I mean it. I suppose that mirror is the magic equivalent of. . . .”

vocabulary.definition: In a way that causes great surprise or wonder.
vocabulary.example: “But this was amazingly strong.”

vocabulary.definition: Entertained or finding something funny.
vocabulary.example: Mr. Saunders was so amused that he had to sit on the blue velvet stool.

vocabulary.definition: Entertaining or funny.
vocabulary.example: Mr. Saunders was so amused that he had to sit on the blue velvet stool.

vocabulary.definition: The joint connecting the foot with the leg.
vocabulary.example: Mr. Saunders came in on Euphemia’s heels, still unable to find evening clothes that covered his wrists and ankles.

vocabulary.definition: In a manner resulting from or revealing anxiety; worriedly.
vocabulary.example: “Is he all right?” Euphemia asked anxiously.

vocabulary.definition: Feeling embarrassed or guilty about something.
vocabulary.example: He sounded a little ashamed. “Now I have to take care to keep each life separately in the safest place I can think of.

vocabulary.definition: Not giving sufficient attention or thought to avoiding harm or errors.
vocabulary.example: “Really?” Cat was pleased and interested. “Nine?” “I did have nine. I’ve only got two now. I was even more careless than Cat,” Chrestomanci said.

vocabulary.definition: An object, act, or saying supposed to have magical power.
vocabulary.example: “There were surely enough charms in this carpet to stop any kind of accident.”

vocabulary.definition: Noticeably happy and optimistic.
vocabulary.example: Cat lay, feeling comfortable and almost cheerful.

vocabulary.definition: An accessory around the neck of a garment.
vocabulary.example: Mr. Saunders came in on Euphemia’s heels, still unable to find evening clothes that covered his wrists and ankles.

vocabulary.definition: Sadness or displeasure caused by the non-fulfilment of one's hopes or expectations.
vocabulary.example: To Janet’s disappointment, Euphemia hurried in with a tray and prevented Chrestomanci explaining how it did work.

vocabulary.definition: Regarded with great fear or apprehension.
vocabulary.example: I had the dreaded umjams when you suddenly flared up.

vocabulary.definition: Showing strong aggressive or intense feelings.
vocabulary.example: Chrestomanci gave him a fierce swig of brandy and then made him drink a cup of sweet tea.

vocabulary.definition: To suddenly become angry or erupt suddenly.
vocabulary.example: I had the dreaded umjams when you suddenly flared up.

vocabulary.definition: Novel; strikingly unusual or different.
vocabulary.example: “Some of the faces were highly original.”

vocabulary.definition: Without delay; immediately.
vocabulary.example: Janet’s ready brain promptly got to work on this.

vocabulary.definition: Uncommon or infrequent.
vocabulary.example: Cat said, “But I haven’t any talents,” and Janet said at the same time, “How rare are these people?”

vocabulary.definition: To destroy or severely damage something.
vocabulary.example: He ruined a suit over you, Cat, and didn’t seem to mind.

vocabulary.definition: To establish in a comfortable new position or place.
vocabulary.example: For a while, everyone was there, settling him in.

vocabulary.definition: Make or become straight or straighter.
vocabulary.example: He got up and turned the mirror straight.

vocabulary.definition: Make or become stronger.
vocabulary.example: Mr. Saunders helped Euphemia straighten the carpet, and then asked if he should strengthen the charms in it.

vocabulary.definition: Put forward for consideration; propose.
vocabulary.example: “Dragons’ blood might do the trick,” he suggested.

vocabulary.definition: A large gulp or swallow of a drink.
vocabulary.example: Chrestomanci gave him a fierce swig of brandy and then made him drink a cup of sweet tea.

vocabulary.definition: A flat, shallow container used to carry things.
vocabulary.example: Euphemia hurried in with a tray and prevented Chrestomanci explaining how it did work.

vocabulary.definition: The joint connecting the hand with the forearm.
vocabulary.example: Mr. Saunders came in on Euphemia’s heels, still unable to find evening clothes that covered his wrists and ankles.
